summary:
The European Witch Trials (c. 1450–1750 AD) were a wave of prosecutions and executions for the crime of witchcraft that resulted in the deaths of an estimated 40,000–60,000 people across Europe and colonial America, with torture, confession extraction, and community denunciation creating self-perpetuating spirals of accusation. The witch trials were not a medieval phenomenon but primarily an early modern one — they intensified during the Reformation era, when both Catholics and Protestants competed to demonstrate their vigilance against the devil, and reached their peak intensity in the century between 1580 and 1680. The Malleus Maleficarum (Hammer of Witches, 1486) — a Dominican inquisitorial manual that provided theological and procedural frameworks for witch trials — was reprinted widely after the printing press made it available.

The trials were geographically concentrated: the Holy Roman Empire, the Swiss cantons, Scotland, and parts of France and Scandinavia saw the highest intensities of persecution, while Italy, Spain, and England (with more rigorous legal standards for evidence) saw proportionally fewer. The accused were disproportionately female (roughly 75% of those executed were women), poor, elderly, and socially marginal — often accused by neighbors in the context of community conflicts, crop failures, or unexplained deaths of livestock and children. The trials declined in the late 17th century as Enlightenment skepticism about supernatural causation, stricter legal standards, and growing judicial reluctance to accept torture-extracted confessions made prosecutions increasingly difficult. The [[event:2331|Salem Witch Trials]] (1692) in Massachusetts were among the last major trials before the practice effectively ended in the Western world.
